sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: An optics lab uses a converging lens with focal length f = +10 cm. An object is placed at distances of 15 cm, 20 cm, and 30 cm from the lens. Students calculate image distances using 1/f = 1/o + 1/i. The corresponding real image distances are 30 cm, 20 cm, and 15 cm. The images are inverted when formed on the opposite side of the lens.
questions.stem: When the object is 20 cm from the lens, what is the image distance?

explanations.A: Correct. For o = 20 cm and f = 10 cm, 1/i = 1/10 − 1/20 = 1/20, so i = 20 cm. The figure also shows this value.
explanations.B: 10 cm is the focal length, not the image distance.
explanations.C: 30 cm corresponds to an object distance of 15 cm.
explanations.D: 40 cm does not satisfy the thin-lens equation here.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Image distance as object distance changes for a converging lens with f = +10 cm.

questions.stem: What is the magnification when o = 30 cm and i = 15 cm?

explanations.C: Correct. Magnification is m = −i/o = −15/30 = −0.5. The negative sign indicates an inverted image, and the magnitude indicates it is half the object’s size.
explanations.A: +0.5 has the correct magnitude but wrong sign for a real inverted image.
explanations.B: −2.0 reverses the ratio of image and object distances.
explanations.D: +2.0 is both wrong in sign and magnitude.

questions.stem: What type of image is formed for the object distances shown?

explanations.B: Correct. All listed object distances exceed the focal length of a converging lens, producing real images on the opposite side. Real images from a single converging lens are inverted.
explanations.A: Virtual upright images occur when the object is inside the focal length.
explanations.C: A real image from this simple lens setup is inverted, not upright.
explanations.D: Virtual images here would not form on the opposite side of the lens.

questions.stem: If the object were placed at the focal point, where would the image form ideally?

explanations.D: Correct. When o = f, 1/i = 1/f − 1/f = 0, corresponding to an image at infinity. Emerging rays are parallel.
explanations.A: The image is not located at the focal point in this limiting case.
explanations.B: 2f is the image distance when the object is at 2f, not at f.
explanations.C: The image does not form at the lens surface.

questions.stem: Why does image distance decrease as object distance increases beyond 2f?

choices.B: The thin-lens equation requires 1/i to increase toward 1/f as 1/o decreases
choices.A: The focal length decreases during the experiment
choices.C: The lens becomes diverging at larger object distances

explanations.B: Correct. As object distance increases, 1/o becomes smaller, so 1/i approaches 1/f. Therefore i approaches f from above and decreases toward 10 cm.
explanations.A: The focal length is fixed at +10 cm.
explanations.C: The lens type does not change when the object moves.
explanations.D: The object remains a real object placed in front of the lens.

questions.passage: Students shine monochromatic light through a single slit onto a distant screen. The distance from slit to screen is 2.0 m. When wavelength is 600 nm and slit width is 0.20 mm, the first dark fringe is measured 6.0 mm from the center. When the slit width is doubled to 0.40 mm, the first minimum moves to 3.0 mm. The class uses y ≈ Lλ/a for small angles.
questions.stem: What happens to the first minimum when slit width doubles?

choices.A: It moves half as far from the center

explanations.A: Correct. For single-slit diffraction, y ≈ Lλ/a. Doubling a halves y, matching the graph from 6.0 mm to 3.0 mm.
explanations.B: A twice-as-far shift would occur if slit width were halved.
explanations.C: The fringe position depends on slit width.
explanations.D: The minimum remains present; its position changes.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Position of the first dark fringe for two single-slit widths using 600 nm light.

questions.stem: Using a = 0.20 mm, what first-minimum distance is predicted?

explanations.A: 0.60 mm is too small by a factor of 10.
explanations.B: 3.0 mm corresponds to the doubled slit width.
explanations.D: 12 mm would result from halving the slit width.

questions.stem: If wavelength were increased while slit width stayed fixed, what would happen to the central maximum width?

explanations.D: Correct. Diffraction spreading increases with wavelength. Since y is proportional to λ, the minima move farther out and the central maximum broadens.
explanations.A: A shorter wavelength would decrease spreading.
explanations.B: Wavelength appears directly in the fringe-position equation.
explanations.C: The central maximum does not vanish simply from increasing wavelength.

questions.stem: Why is diffraction more pronounced for smaller apertures?

choices.B: Light stops behaving as a wave
choices.D: The speed of light changes drastically in air

explanations.A: Correct. Diffraction angle scales with λ/a. When aperture size a gets closer to the wavelength scale, spreading becomes larger.
explanations.B: Diffraction is specifically a wave phenomenon.
explanations.C: The frequency of light does not become zero.
explanations.D: The speed of light in air is not the explanation for the aperture-size effect.

questions.stem: Which condition best supports the small-angle approximation used in the passage?

choices.C: The fringe distance is much smaller than the screen distance
choices.A: The slit width is larger than the screen distance
choices.B: The wavelength is measured in nanometers

explanations.C: Correct. The approximation sinθ ≈ tanθ ≈ y/L is valid when y is much smaller than L. Here millimeter fringe distances are much smaller than a 2.0 m screen distance.
explanations.A: The slit width is much smaller than the screen distance, not larger.
explanations.B: Nanometer units alone do not justify the approximation.
explanations.D: Monochromatic light helps produce clear fringes but does not define the small-angle approximation.
